title: Week 11 Worklog
content:
Week 11 Objectives: Start the IRMS project with the team and align on scope, responsibilities, and overall architecture. Focus my individual contribution on AWS infrastructure, AWS SAM, Cognito, API Gateway, Lambda, and DynamoDB. Synchronize the Proposal, Workshop, and Worklog with the real project timeline. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 1 - Starting the IRMS project
- Work: I joined the team discussion to officially start the IRMS project. The team aligned on the scope of an information security incident management system, while I focused on AWS infrastructure, deployment flow, and how the project could be documented as a workshop. I reviewed the main requirements: incident management, timeline tracking, evidence upload, report generation, and alert handling. I then listed the AWS services needed for the project, including Cognito, API Gateway, Lambda, DynamoDB, S3 Evidence Store, EventBridge, SNS, Secrets Manager, CloudWatch, and CloudFront. I also separated the areas I would handle directly from the areas that required coordination with frontend and backend teammates.
- Knowledge: A team project needs clear scope and responsibility boundaries early; otherwise, implementation and documentation can drift quickly.
- Result: The team had a clearer implementation direction, and I had an AWS infrastructure and documentation checklist for the following days.
- Lesson: There were many possible extensions at first, so we had to prioritize the core features to fit the internship schedule. 29/06/2026 29/06/2026 IRMS source code, AWS Documentation, team notes 2 - Requirement analysis and technical assignment
- Work: I worked with the team to break the IRMS use cases into smaller implementation flows. My deeper focus was user authentication with Cognito, REST routing through API Gateway, business logic in Lambda, and data storage in DynamoDB. I helped describe the data flow for Incident CRUD, Timeline, Evidence Upload, and Report Generation. For each flow, I wrote down the input, output, related AWS services, and testing points. I also discussed with the frontend teammate to align the endpoints, response format, and how the frontend should send the JWT in the Authorization header.
- Knowledge: In a serverless project, the API contract is important because frontend code, Lambda functions, and DynamoDB access all depend on the same data structure.
- Result: The work items became clearer, which helped me prepare the SAM template and workshop documentation closer to the actual implementation.
- Lesson: Some requirements were still broad, so I needed to turn them into concrete checklists instead of relying on assumptions. 30/06/2026 30/06/2026 IRMS source code, AWS Documentation, team notes 3 - Finalizing the overall architecture
- Work: I focused on the architecture diagram and the request flow from the React frontend to backend services. The team agreed on a serverless direction: React + Vite frontend hosted with S3/CloudFront, backend through API Gateway, Cognito Authorizer, Lambda, and DynamoDB, with evidence files stored in S3 through presigned URLs. I checked the connection points between GuardDuty, EventBridge, Lambda Alert Handler, and SNS so the alerting section would be documented correctly. For AI Assistant, I clearly noted that Lambda reads the secret from Secrets Manager and calls the Groq API, avoiding API keys in the frontend or public documentation. I also updated notes to separate implemented architecture from future enhancements.
- Knowledge: Architecture is not only a diagram; it must match how requests, credentials, logs, and data actually move through the system.
- Result: The IRMS architecture was finalized for consistent use in the Proposal, Workshop, and Worklog.
- Lesson: If service names and arrows are not aligned early, later documentation pages can easily contradict each other. 01/07/2026 01/07/2026 IRMS source code, AWS Documentation, team notes 4 - Preparing AWS SAM and infrastructure
- Work: I started preparing the AWS SAM structure for the backend infrastructure. I reviewed the resources by group: Cognito for authentication, API Gateway for REST APIs, Lambda for business logic, DynamoDB for incident/timeline/user/evidence metadata, S3 for evidence, and EventBridge/SNS for alerts. I validated the template with sam validate, reviewed Lambda IAM roles, and checked environment variables such as table names, bucket names, secret name, and AI provider. I also recorded the steps that needed to appear in the workshop, including AWS CLI/SAM CLI installation, profile configuration, build, validation, and deployment. Commands: sam validate sam build sam deploy --guided
- Knowledge: SAM helps keep infrastructure in code, but variable names and IAM permissions must be clear to avoid deployment errors.
- Result: The backend infrastructure baseline was ready for the team to continue Lambda implementation and API testing.
- Lesson: Small template or IAM mistakes can block deployment, so validating early is better than waiting until the end. 02/07/2026 02/07/2026 IRMS source code, AWS Documentation, team notes 5 - Initial documentation synchronization
- Work: I started synchronizing the Proposal and Workshop with the agreed IRMS architecture. The main goal was to keep the Vietnamese and English versions equivalent in content, with only the language being different. I reviewed the Introduction, Environment Setup, and Infrastructure Configuration sections to avoid mismatched bilingual content. I also recorded items that needed further validation in the following week, such as Cognito JWT, API Gateway CORS, Lambda logs, DynamoDB key design, S3 presigned URLs, and CloudFront deployment. Items that were not fully tested were marked for confirmation instead of being written as completed.
- Knowledge: Technical documentation should be updated alongside implementation, because leaving everything until the end makes it difficult to remember real issues and fixes accurately.
- Result: Worklog, Proposal, and Workshop started following the same project timeline from the week that includes July 1.
- Lesson: The worklog must describe my personal contribution in a five-member team, not present the whole system as work I completed alone. 03/07/2026 03/07/2026 IRMS source code, AWS Documentation, team notes Week 11 Achievements: 29/06/2026: The team had a clearer implementation direction, and I had an AWS infrastructure and documentation checklist for the following days. There were many possible extensions at first, so we had to prioritize the core features to fit the internship schedule. 30/06/2026: The work items became clearer, which helped me prepare the SAM template and workshop documentation closer to the actual implementation. Some requirements were still broad, so I needed to turn them into concrete checklists instead of relying on assumptions. 01/07/2026: The IRMS architecture was finalized for consistent use in the Proposal, Workshop, and Worklog. If service names and arrows are not aligned early, later documentation pages can easily contradict each other. 02/07/2026: The backend infrastructure baseline was ready for the team to continue Lambda implementation and API testing. Small template or IAM mistakes can block deployment, so validating early is better than waiting until the end. 03/07/2026: Worklog, Proposal, and Workshop started following the same project timeline from the week that includes July 1. The worklog must describe my personal contribution in a five-member team, not present the whole system as work I completed alone.

title: Week 12 Worklog
content:
Week 12 Objectives: Complete the remaining IRMS work: frontend integration, deployment, testing, cost analysis, and cleanup. Check CloudFront, S3 hosting, APIs, authentication, and the main demo flows. Finalize the project report, workshop, and internship documents with a consistent format. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 1 - Backend and AWS infrastructure testing
- Work: I checked the backend and infrastructure parts implemented by the team, focusing on Cognito, API Gateway, Lambda, DynamoDB, S3 Evidence Store, EventBridge, SNS, and CloudWatch Logs. My main responsibility was to ensure the AWS services connected correctly with the flow described in the workshop. I tested Cognito login, retrieved a JWT, and called protected APIs. When I encountered 401 or CORS issues, I reviewed the authorizer, routes, stage deployment, and response headers. For Lambda, I checked logs to confirm that requests reached the correct functions, data was written to the right DynamoDB tables, and errors were returned clearly. I also tested evidence upload through presigned URLs to ensure files went directly to S3 while metadata was stored separately.
- Knowledge: Serverless testing needs to trace the flow from browser to API Gateway, Lambda, database, and logs because errors can happen at any layer.
- Result: The main flows, including authentication, Incident CRUD, Timeline, and Evidence Upload, became more stable for end-to-end testing.
- Lesson: CORS and JWT errors can look similar at first, so authentication errors, routing errors, and backend errors should be debugged separately. 06/07/2026 06/07/2026 IRMS source code, AWS Documentation, team notes 2 - Deployment, CloudFront, and end-to-end testing
- Work: I collaborated with the team to test the React + Vite frontend after build and deployment. My focus was API base URL configuration, S3 hosting, CloudFront distribution, cache behavior, and invalidation after frontend updates. I checked npm run build, the dist/ folder, asset paths, and browser console errors. After uploading the frontend to S3 and accessing it through CloudFront, I tested login, incident creation, status update, evidence upload, timeline viewing, and report generation. When the browser still loaded an old bundle, I created a CloudFront invalidation for /* and used hard refresh to compare the result. Commands: npm run build aws s3 sync dist/ s3://<frontend-bucket> --delete aws cloudfront create-invalidation --distribution-id <distribution-id> --paths "/*"
- Knowledge: Frontend deployment is not only uploading files; it also requires checking cache, base URLs, HTTPS behavior, and real backend calls.
- Result: The frontend could run more reliably through CloudFront and call the main APIs.
- Lesson: Without cache invalidation, viewers may still see old UI or JavaScript even after the source has been updated. 08/07/2026 08/07/2026 IRMS source code, AWS Documentation, team notes 3 - Completing the Workshop according to the implementation flow
- Work: I updated Workshop sections to match the final IRMS flow: Introduction, Environment Setup, Infrastructure Configuration, Application Development, Deployment and Testing, Frontend Integration and Web Deployment, and Results/Cost/Cleanup. The goal was to summarize sections without losing important technical content. I reviewed places that previously had joined paragraphs or Vietnamese text without accents in the English version. Long technical flows such as Lambda, S3 presigned URLs, EventBridge, Secrets Manager, and AI Assistant were split into bullets, tables, or code blocks for readability. I also checked the numbering for 5.1, 5.2, 5.3, and their child sections so the sidebar displayed correctly. Sensitive information such as passwords, access keys, secret keys, tokens, and API keys was replaced with placeholders.
- Knowledge: A good workshop must follow the order a reader actually performs the work while also keeping examples secure.
- Result: Workshop content became easier to read, more synchronized between Vietnamese and English, and closer to the AWS work I contributed to.
- Lesson: Documentation copied from working notes often has broken formatting or missing context, so each page needs to be checked carefully. 10/07/2026 10/07/2026 IRMS source code, AWS Documentation, team notes 4 - Synchronizing the report, Proposal, and Worklog
- Work: I reviewed the Proposal, Worklog, Events, Self-Assessment, Sharing and Feedback, and README so the content stayed consistent with IRMS. I checked the architecture diagram, AWS service names, AI provider, internship dates, student information, and internal links. I replaced the architecture diagram in both Proposal and Workshop, then checked image paths under static/images. For the English version, I fixed sentences that still contained Vietnamese or sounded unnatural. For the Vietnamese version, I corrected spelling, paragraph breaks, and tone so it read more like a personal internship report. I also reviewed the Worklog to make sure it did not claim that I completed the whole project alone.
- Knowledge: An internship report needs consistency across sections because a mentor can compare the Proposal, Workshop, Worklog, and final result.
- Result: The report became more consistent in timeline, architecture, personal contribution, and AWS terminology.
- Lesson: When editing bilingual files, both meaning and formatting must be checked, not only sentence-by-sentence translation. 14/07/2026 14/07/2026 IRMS source code, AWS Documentation, team notes 5 - Completing the project report
- Work: I completed the final review according to the actual progress up to July 21, 2026. The work included checking Hugo build, GitHub Pages live demo, main page rendering, internal links, architecture images, sidebar numbering, and bilingual content. I ran a production build with the GitHub Pages base URL, reviewed Hugo warnings, and confirmed there were no Markdown or front matter errors breaking the site. I also checked for sensitive information one last time to avoid publishing login emails, passwords, access keys, secret keys, tokens, or API keys. The final adjustment was updating the Worklog timeline so the early weeks represent AWS learning and labs, while the IRMS project starts from the week that includes July 1. Commands: hugo --minify --baseURL https://mxt2003.github.io/Internship/
- Knowledge: Completing the report is not only writing content; it also requires checking build output, links, images, sensitive data, and consistency across the whole website.
- Result: The report reflected the actual timeline, my personal role in the team, and the completed project status up to July 21, 2026.
- Lesson: Near the end, fixing one section can easily affect another, so a checklist-based review is safer than relying only on visual inspection. 21/07/2026 21/07/2026 IRMS source code, AWS Documentation, team notes Week 12 Achievements: 06/07/2026: The main flows, including authentication, Incident CRUD, Timeline, and Evidence Upload, became more stable for end-to-end testing. CORS and JWT errors can look similar at first, so authentication errors, routing errors, and backend errors should be debugged separately. 08/07/2026: The frontend could run more reliably through CloudFront and call the main APIs. Without cache invalidation, viewers may still see old UI or JavaScript even after the source has been updated. 10/07/2026: Workshop content became easier to read, more synchronized between Vietnamese and English, and closer to the AWS work I contributed to.
